speaker: interviewer
text: The date is February the 13th, 1996. I'm interviewing Max Grosblat. My name is Sol Awend, A-W-E-N-D. And we are in St. Louis Park, Minnesota, United States of America. Could you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Max Grosblat. Last name is G-R-O-S-B-L-A-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dubno, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dubno is D-U-B-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1922, March 7, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, I'm 73 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your home in Dubno, what your mother and father's kitchen was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was strictly orthodox. We belonged to a shul that was a Hasidic shul. Hasidic shul-- even it was called Hasidic shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the rabbi was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember anything about your house, your home there in Dubno? What kind of a place it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had living quarters plus a working shop where my dad used to work, in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your mom do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mom was our housekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember any memories of home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a nice life, quiet and happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had four brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my sister was Pearl. She was born 1926, 1924, two years after I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came Pearl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came Pearl, and then came Shlomo. And Shlomo was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the third. After Shlomo, then came Benjamin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any stories you can remember about your brothers or your sisters, when you were a young fellow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when it used to rain outside, we used to run out in the rain and play outside. We loved to play in the rain. Well, of course, Mother didn't approve of it, because she had to change our clothes. That was a lot of fun. Most important activity that I remember for me was swimming. We-- we lived close to a river. And I used to go down there every day and swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting when you were how old? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: About seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you compare the river? Was it-- not like the Mississippi River?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not as big as the Mississippi. It was quite a small river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sometimes the whole family went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious upbringing? Did you go to Talmud Torah, to Hebrew school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went. I went to cheder and then I went to yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah wasn't as elaborate as they make it right now. I was called to the Torah. And I recited a-- the portion of the Torah that I was supposed to recite. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had some refreshments. And there was no fancy dinner, such things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you like to do as a young boy? You were a swimmer. Were you involved in sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids in our neighborhood formed a group, we played soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the street address in your neighborhood where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [NON-ENGLISH], number two. [NON-ENGLISH] means not quite a full street, just like a little, small street-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: It was mostly, probably all, Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were all Jewish there. Also, across the street from my home, there was a Polish lady and her husband, Larry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember them very well. Because when I came back after the war, I stayed there for-- for a little while, until I went to emigrate to Germany. I stayed in their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's been a long time. And I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we move on. You-- you like to play sports. You love to go swimming. And life is good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to see a change, that things became different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right after 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Russians came in. And they occupied our area. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 1941, the Germans came in. And they occupied our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: June, I think, 19-- 1939. I think 1941, also June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it was like that day? Or any incidents about when that all started? Would you like a glass of water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened around that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people were actually very sad and crying, because when the Russians came in, they did not allow any free enterprise. And most of the people that lived there were small grocery man, small shop man. They were afraid to lose all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the economy in Dubno was a good economy for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very good economy, because Dubno was in a very rich agricultural area. We had fruit, and poultry, and eggs that we shipped abroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the Jews do in that economy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews were go between the farmer and the buyers in the other country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were packing the eggs, and sending them out, packing the poultry, and packing fruit. And they sent them all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family didn't necessarily get involved in that so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family came from Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family came from Germany in the 1880s. When the Crusaders came out to go to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] free-- free the Holy Land from the infidels, they-- they started on the path of killing the Jews in their path. So my family ran away from Germany. We settled in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And never went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they never went back. We settled in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moving ahead towards the beginning of the war, what did your mom and dad do when that all happened? What was the mood like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very sad situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember of the incidents that happened? What was your dad saying at the time? Any thoughts come to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we heard a lot of bad stories of what was going on in-- in Germany and in Russia. And we were all fearful that that same thing is going to happen in our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when, in the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of stories were you hearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said they were taking people. And people disappeared. Nobody knew where they where. They just disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mom and dad decide what to do or any plans? Or what were their thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they wait it out and see what happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the war begins. You hear news that's very distressing. And what are your brothers and sisters, what are you doing at the time? Are you still going to school or did that get interrupted?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians came in, they closed the cheder. They closed the yeshiva. It was interrupted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there very many acts of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not only doing the war, it was even before the war was a lot of anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember when it was about 5 o'clock on Sundays. We had to stay away from the churches, because when they came out of the service, they start hitting any Jews they saw. So we have to stay away from the-- from the area of-- of churches, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it any worse around Easter time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the worst was around Christmas time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Jews were accused of killing Christ.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the war gets worse. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you start hearing the stories. What happened next? Was there any decision that your parents made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we had two wars there. The first war was Russia invaded our area. And the second war was where-- when Germany invaded our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians invaded, you had to deal with the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were they anti-Semitic? And were they brutal? How did they react to you and how did you react to them? What did they do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To us personally, they didn't do very much. But to the people that had small-- small businesses, they-- locked them up-- they couldn't have any more businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They locked them up. They locked the business up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: People had to make a living best way they knew how.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do for making a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was still working as a tailor. And he was still in a synagogue singing during the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you? What are you doing about this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was harder and harder to make a living. So I took the responsibility. I said I can somehow make some money. So that-- And I used to go from our town to Lemberg. Lemberg, it's Lviv. We-- over there used to be a lot of products that you couldn't get them in Dubno at that time. I used to go by train and buy a lot of-- a lot of things that we couldn't get them in Dubno. Then bring them and sell them on-- on the market. That's where we had some income, and we could exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you buy from and who did you sell to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought it from the people that were selling it in Lemberg. There's a market, open market. So I went there. And sometimes I bought some vodka, sometimes I bought some soap, sometimes I bought some other items that I know they're scarce in Dubno. And when I brought them back, I sold them at a profit. And I gave the money to my parents to have enough money to support us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go by yourself or did you with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with some of my friends who were doing the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you brought back a shipment, how big was a shipment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not too big, a backpack. A backpack, we packed it so we could carry it with us all the time, because sometimes they were chasing us. We had to run. So we made backpacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was chasing you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians are in Dubno in your area for about two years, like 1939 to 1941. And during that time it wasn't necessarily bad yet for the Jews. Or was the general population, even the Ukrainians, bad to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians didn't give us too much trouble at that time, except for the people that were small merchants. They locked them out of their business. That was a terrific blow to the people, took away their income. People had to sell their prized possessions in order to exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it did. But we didn't have any business. We-- my father was working as a tailor. And that they couldn't take away from him, because the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] population around Dubno was mostly Ukrainians, and Polish, and Germans, and Russians. So when they wanted something, a new suit, a new coat, they had to come to my dad. My dad should make it for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your dad did OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The advanced German-- German militaries that came into our town, they were all on motorcycles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stood there and watched them, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all on motorcycles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any warning they were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have any warning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden you start seeing these motorcycles.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of a sudden, we start seeing those German soldiers on motorcycles, coming into town. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color were their uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite a deep blue, it's light blueish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were part of what, the Wehrmacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of day was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was about 1 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember the day. But I know we all ran out there to watch them come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had to live with it. And they gave-- they gave orders to-- to where we lived. They only gave us about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] four blocks to live in, between east and west, and seven blocks between north and south. That area was for-- a ghetto. We had to build a 10-foot-- 10 feet fence around that area. And we couldn't get out from there. And they had to go through guards to come in and go out. They posted guards around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden your house is not your house anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people lived with you. Do you remember who moved in with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my aunt and her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Conditions started getting worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no food. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When you went out-- out from the ghetto on the street, you had to be careful not to walk on the sidewalk. Because if you walked on the sidewalk, they'd just throw you off the sidewalk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had to wear a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that? [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Did it make you angry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it did make me angry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened? Did things start to progressively get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worse, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to take our prized possessions and trade them for some food. The farmers used to come to the-- to the ghetto, not inside the ghetto. We used to trade them. They were on the outside of the ghetto. We were on the inside of the ghetto. We used to trade our prized possessions for some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me. You mention prized possessions, like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a gold watch and a gold ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you're now suffering with being in a ghetto, not having enough to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a day like like that? What did you do all day long?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took the-- all able-bodied to work. We had to go to a certain designated area. And they came and they picked out 10 or 20 people. And they took them to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a part of the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have four minutes left on this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to the forest, then, and cut down trees? Or what kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans first came, they had me working making roads, asphalt. We had to make better roads for them that they could advance faster. We had cobblestones and that wasn't good enough for them. They had to have a smooth road so they can advance faster.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us out. And we worked on the roads until later on, when the Gendarmerie came. Gendamerie was a police. They were sent to take care of law and order in our town.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came in, they needed people to work for them. So they sent me over to the Gendamerie there-- myself and my cousin, were-- we were using wood for cooking and for heating. We had to sew it and cut it to a certain size, so it would fit in the ovens and the stoves. So my cousin and I were sent to work for the Gendarmerie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
